So this is directly from Facebook. Our goal is to match the right ad to the right person at the right time. This is different than a traditional auction, because the winner isn't the ad with the highest monetary bid. But the ad that creates the most total value. You know, just I just want to emphasize that Facebook are trying to give the user the best experience as possible. It's not about you as an advertiser and how much money you can make out of Facebook. It's about how Facebook can keep the user happy. That's the bottom line for all of this. If Facebook doesn't have happy users, and you know, there have been lots of cases recently and over the years where people haven't been happy, then people start to leave Facebook and that damages Facebook far more than you spending less money on them. So Facebook's number one priority is the user and the user experience. And then it's to make money and it's to make money from you to monetize the user as well. Now, if your pixel is optimized, and it's got lots of history behind it, you know, you've been running a store or a website for two, three years, then there's a good chance that people, Facebook will find the people that you're after. But actually, if you can start to layer targeting on top, it will give you a better chance of serving the right creative to the right person. And that's what this second part is about on ad relevance. So getting your targeting right plus making the relevant is crucial to cutting through in the auction. So let's take a beauty product, for example, you could target the whole of one country, and you might say this is just females only, and I'm just going to push this out there. And even even if your pixel is optimized, and it's been running for years and picked up lots of data, if your product has a different appeal to younger women versus older women, just splitting it by age and then splitting the message has such a big impact on performance. So if you're selling some kind of beauty facial product and for young women, it helps them to remain looking young. For older women, it makes their skin feel younger. Then all of a sudden you've got two creative angles to play with. But the key thing here is splitting your audience to make sure the ad and the creative is as relevant as possible. That makes such a huge difference to your click through rate, your cost per click and your website conversion as well. So they're all the key things that come together to give you the best performance. The third part of this is landing page experience. So if you're running a lead gen ads on Facebook, you're keeping people in Facebook, or you're using mobile app install, there's different aspects of the landing page experience that come in play. But if you've got a website, then it's all about how long does it take your page to load. Facebook does take this into account because that affects the user value. If someone lands on your site and it takes two, three seconds to load and different elements are loading, people are just going to leave, which leads on to the next important part, which is the bounce rate. Facebook does track the bounce rate and attributes that back into the auction as well. So if people land on your site and it's a completely different message to the one that you had in your ad, and there's a disconnect, there's no congruency between your ad and your landing page, people will bounce back into Facebook and Facebook measure that as a negative signal against your ad as well. So making sure your landing page is on point is so, so important as well. And then the kind of sales funnel experience. So Facebook will be able to tell how long people spend on your site. They'll be able to tell how many pages they go through, which page they fall off, how many times they've visited. Just think about it for a second. You have installed your pixel on your website and more than likely you've got that pixel installed on every single page on your website. Whatever happens on your website, whatever Google Analytics picks up, Facebook has access to that as well. So Facebook will know whether you're running an ad or not at that moment in time, exactly who is on your website and what they're looking at. The creative phishing strategy is finding creatives that are going to work best with your audience. So let's say you've got an established website, an established Facebook account and you have some audiences that are working, let's say a 2% lookalike of all your customers and you're experiencing creative fatigue and you need to refresh up your creatives. Most people would then start to test new creatives out. This graduation framework is all about being two steps ahead. So it's about having audiences and creatives ready for you when you need them. So there's no waiting around and saying, you know, I now need a creative for this. It's expired out. What you want to be doing is through the graduation framework, be testing in the background. There should be no reason at all that you are never testing anything on Facebook. So your testing should literally be seven days of the week.
